title: Why the return of boredom means your nervous system is finally safe
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
You might call it emptiness. I call it a homecoming. 

 I was sitting on my porch last Saturday. Nothing was happening. No phone in my hand. No podcast in...
htmlContent:
You might call it emptiness. I call it a homecoming.

I was sitting on my porch last Saturday. Nothing was happening. No phone in my hand. No podcast in my ears. No urgent task clawing at my attention. Just the sound of a distant lawnmower and the way the light fell through the oak leaves. And I felt this strange, almost unsettling quiet in my chest. Not the heavy, hollow kind of quiet I used to know. But something softer. Something that felt almost like... boredom.

Honestly, it scared me at first. I almost reached for my phone. I almost filled the space with noise. But I didn't. I just sat there and let myself feel bored. And that's when I realized something important. Boredom, real boredom, the kind you can actually tolerate without your skin crawling, might be one of the clearest signs that your nervous system is finally starting to feel safe.

Stay with me here. I know that sounds strange. We've been trained to see boredom as a problem. Something to fix. Something to escape. But what if boredom is actually a signal, a message from your body that the emergency is over? What if the return of boredom means you're finally coming back online, back into the present moment, back into your own life?

Let's talk about what's really happening when you can't stand to be bored.

The nervous system's addiction to emergency

Here's the thing about a traumatized nervous system. It doesn't know how to rest. It's been wired for survival, for constant scanning, for detecting threats and responding to them. This isn't a choice. It's a biological adaptation. Your body learned that safety was not guaranteed. That stillness could mean danger. That if you stopped moving, stopped doing, stopped distracting yourself, something terrible might catch up to you.

Dr. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, explains that our nervous system has a hierarchy of states. There's the ventral vagal state, which is our safe, social engagement state. That's where we can be calm, connected, and present. Then there's the sympathetic state, our fight or flight system. And then there's the dorsal vagal state, our freeze or collapse response. Here's what most people don't realize. Many of us have been living in a mix of sympathetic activation and dorsal collapse for so long that we've forgotten what ventral safety actually feels like.

When you're stuck in chronic hyperarousal, your brain is constantly searching for stimulation. It needs to be busy. It needs to be solving problems. It needs to be scrolling, planning, worrying, consuming. Because stillness feels like threat. Silence feels like the moment before something bad happens. Boredom feels like a trap.

I used to fill every empty moment with noise. Podcasts while I showered. Music while I cooked. A show while I ate. Social media while I waited in line. I couldn't just be. I couldn't just sit. The silence was too loud. It was full of memories I didn't want to touch. Full of feelings I didn't know how to hold.

So I kept moving. Kept consuming. Kept the engine running even when there was nowhere to go. And I called it productivity. I called it staying informed. I called it self-care. But really, it was just survival.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She writes about how trauma fragments our experience of time. How it keeps us stuck in the past or anxiously projecting into the future. Boredom, real boredom, requires being fully in the present. And for a traumatized nervous system, the present has often been the most dangerous place to be.

What boredom actually asks of you

Boredom is not the absence of stimulation. It's the presence of something else. Something quieter. Something that doesn't demand your attention but waits for it. Boredom asks you to sit with yourself. Not the version of yourself that's performing, achieving, or escaping. But the version that's just there. Breathing. Existing. Being.

And for many of us, that's terrifying. Because when you stop running, you start feeling. And the feelings that come up when the noise fades, they're not always pleasant. There's grief. There's anger. There's loneliness. There's the raw, unprocessed ache of everything you've been carrying.

So you reach for your phone again. You turn on another podcast. You find something to fix, organize, or . Anything to avoid the quiet. Anything to avoid the boredom.

But here's the thing. That avoidance is keeping you stuck. It's keeping your nervous system in a state of low-grade emergency. Because as long as you're running from boredom, you're telling your body that stillness is unsafe. That being alone with yourself is dangerous. That the present moment is something to be escaped.

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. He talks about how recovery requires learning to inhabit your body again. To feel your sensations without judgment. To be present with what is, even when what is feels uncomfortable. Boredom is a doorway to that. It's an invitation to practice being present without needing anything to change.

I remember the first time I sat through a full ten minutes of boredom without reaching for a distraction. It felt like my skin was crawling. My mind kept throwing up reasons to move. "You should check your email. You should start that load of laundry. You should text your friend back." But I just sat there. And after a while, the crawling stopped. My breath got deeper. My shoulders dropped. And I felt this weird, unfamiliar sensation. Peace. Not excitement. Not happiness. Just peace.

Wild, right?

The difference between boredom and numbness

Now, I need to be careful here. Because there's a difference between boredom and the kind of emptiness that comes from dissociation. And if you've experienced complex trauma, you probably know exactly what I'm talking about.

Dissociation feels like being underwater. Like the world is happening on the other side of a thick glass wall. You're not bored. You're gone. You're checked out. Your nervous system has gone into dorsal vagal shutdown, and you're not really present enough to be bored. That's not what I'm talking about.

Real boredom has an edge to it. A restlessness. A sense that you want to do something but nothing seems worth doing. It's not empty. It's full of potential energy. Full of the possibility of choosing where to direct your attention. It's a sign that your nervous system has come back online enough to feel the discomfort of the present moment without immediately needing to escape it.

Think about it. When you're deeply dissociated, you don't feel bored. You feel nothing. You feel flat. You feel like a ghost haunting your own life. The return of boredom means you're coming back into your body. You're feeling the friction of being alive again. And that friction, uncomfortable as it is, is a sign of healing.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Dr. Arielle Schwartz. It walks you through exercises for grounding, regulation, and building tolerance for being present. It's not about fixing yourself. It's about learning to be with yourself.

How to practice welcoming boredom back

If you're reading this and thinking, "Okay, but how do I actually do this? How do I stop reaching for distraction?" I want you to know that this is a process. It's not about forcing yourself to be bored. It's about building capacity, slowly, gently, to tolerate more presence.

Start small. Like, really small. Put your phone in another room for five minutes. Sit on your couch and just look out the window. Don't try to meditate. Don't try to breathe in any special way. Just sit. And notice what comes up. Notice the urge to move. Notice the stories your mind tells you about why you need to be doing something. Notice the discomfort in your body. And then, if you can, just stay there for a few more seconds.

That's it. That's the practice. Staying with the discomfort just a little longer than you think you can. Not to punish yourself. But to teach your nervous system that boredom is not dangerous. That you can survive the quiet. That nothing bad happens when you stop running.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She offers specific exercises for coming into the ventral vagal state, for finding your anchor in the present moment. It's a gentle, grounded approach that doesn't ask you to bypass your trauma but to meet it with curiosity.

You might find that as you practice, the boredom starts to shift. It might turn into curiosity. Or sadness. Or a memory you haven't thought about in years. That's okay. That's part of the process. Your nervous system is finally allowing itself to feel what it's been holding at bay.

And sometimes, the boredom just stays boredom. And that's okay too. You don't have to make it mean anything. You don't have to turn it into a spiritual experience. You can just let it be what it is. A quiet moment. A pause. A breath between all the doing.

The return is not a destination

I want to be honest with you about something. This doesn't mean you'll never feel the urge to escape again. I still reach for my phone sometimes. I still get anxious in the quiet. I still have days when my nervous system is screaming for stimulation. That's not a failure. That's being human.

But the return of boredom, even for a few minutes, is a sign that something has shifted. It's a sign that your body is starting to trust that the present moment is safe enough to be in. That you don't have to be on high alert anymore. That the emergency, whatever it was, is over.

And that's a big deal. That's not a small thing. That's your nervous system saying, "I'm ready to come home."

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She writes about how we can learn to hold our experience with compassion, even the parts we've been running from. It's a beautiful companion for this work.

So if you find yourself bored today, don't reach for your phone right away. Don't fill the space. Just sit with it for a moment. Let it be there. Let yourself be there. Notice what it feels like to not need anything to change. Even for a few seconds. Even for a breath.

That boredom you're feeling? It might just be your nervous system saying it's safe enough to be alive. Right here. Right now. With nothing to fix and nowhere to run.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Dr. Arielle Schwartz
  • Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach
